card.intuition: Learn dense word vectors by training a shallow network to predict a word from its context (CBOW) or context from a word (Skip-gram). CBOW: average context embeddings → predict centre word via softmax. The Distributional hypothesis: words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings → king - man + woman ≈ queen in embedding space.
card.analogy: Learning the meaning of a word from its neighbours: if you always see 'bank' next to 'river' and 'water', you infer its aquatic meaning — if instead near 'money' and 'loan', the financial meaning. Word2Vec automates this by adjusting vectors so context predicts word correctly.
card.time_complexity: O(T · W · d) training (T tokens, W context window, d embedding dim)
card.space_complexity: O(V · d) for vocabulary V, dimension d
when_to_use: Learn semantic word representations from large unlabelled text corpora
Downstream NLP as initialisation (pre-BERT era, still useful for small corpora)
Analogy tasks: word arithmetic in embedding space
gotchas: Negative sampling essential for efficiency — full softmax over V is too slow
Rare words get poor embeddings — use subword embeddings (fastText) for morphologically rich languages
Static embeddings: one vector per word regardless of context — contextual embeddings (BERT) are superior

exercise.prompt: Implement the CBOW forward pass: average context embeddings to predict the centre word.
hints: The output score for word v is the dot product of the averaged hidden vector h with the v-th column of W_out: Σⱼ W_out[j][v] · h[j].
exercise.test_code:
import math
cbow = CBOW(vocab_size=10, emb_dim=4)

# Forward pass
h, probs = cbow.forward([0, 2, 4])
assert len(h) == 4
assert len(probs) == 10
assert abs(sum(probs) - 1.0) < 1e-9, 'Probs must sum to 1'
assert all(p > 0 for p in probs)

# Different contexts give different predictions
_, p2 = cbow.forward([1, 3, 5])
assert probs != p2
print('PASS')
